summary: Cathy O'Neil announces her second book 'Shame Machine: an owner's manual,' a follow-up to Weapons of Math Destruction with the same editor and publisher. The book will explore shame as a social mechanism—examining who profits from it, who gains power through it, and how it operates in the age of big data. She traces her interest to diverse experiences including insurance battles over bariatric surgery, Twitter interactions, and the value-added model used to score teachers. The book represents a more personal direction than her previous work, which is why she hasn't been testing ideas publicly on her blog. She's now transitioning from planning to active writing, with a tentative January 2021 release date.
key_points: The book examines shame as social infrastructure: who profits from it, who maintains power through it, and when it's valid versus cruel
O'Neil sees shame proliferating in both political strategies and interpersonal interactions, amplified by big data
Her interest stems from personal experiences (insurance/bariatric surgery) and observed injustices (teacher scoring with value-added models, Twitter pile-ons)
This project is more personal than Weapons of Math Destruction, leading her to develop ideas privately rather than on her blog
She frames shame as 'the informal glue that holds our system together'
money_quotes: When, why, and how do we shame each other? Who profits from shame? Who maintains power or gains power through shame?
When is shame valid, and when is it simply mean and cruel?
It seems like shame is the informal glue that holds our system together.
So naturally I started nerding out bigtime.
This time it's quite a bit more personal, so I've been hesitant to write about it openly while I was still thinking it through.
key_insight: Shame functions as invisible social infrastructure—systematically deployed by those in power, amplified by big data, and worthy of the same rigorous analysis we apply to algorithms.
